proposal_text:
Proposal number: 1333885

Proposal: LET'S WALK! - climate change awareness day event

Summary


Purpose: helping to rise the climate change awareness our world so urgently needs. 

Suggested actions: During that day we encourage people to walk and to:

leave their cars at home,
skip the bus,
avoid the subway and
turn to their own bodies in order to move around the city, by walking, by using their bicycles, their rollers, their skates.
If you are not lucky enough to be living near your work space you can still collaborate by doing home office and other activities such as walking to the grocery or the club.
Using a smartphone app we intend to track how much people walk during the day of the event. This information will then be published by social media and traditional media. The data provided by the app will also be translate in the reductions already mentioned, calculating savings in relationship  with the kilometrers walked and avoiding using transportation during that day.The application may use a similar alogorithm that "calculate your ecological footprint" uses, that is available at the Government web page [14].
Because of the altruistic spirit of the cause, we expect to have support from the local ONG´s, health organizations and the government.     
Main value generation loops v1.0.png


Short term Impacts: we expect to save energy and money, GHG emissions, and we will contribute to build sustainable habits, for a healthy environment in the war against the dangers of a sedentary lifestyle.  

Long term impacts:

Since the proposal is to make people aware of how important it is to care about the planet. More climate change aware people would naturally support the following initiatives:

Recycling: We forecast that it could increase by 10 %,
 
Energy savings: recommendations use increment by 10 %
 
Distributed solar photovoltaic electricity law: is approved and covers 5 % of city demand.
 
Electric buses: their use is implemented in 2 % of city bus lines.
Is this proposal for a practice or a project?
Practice

What actions do you propose?
a) The main thrust of “Let’s Walk” is walking, because Buenos Aires has a high population density, and distances might be shorter than in other cities, so walking to work is a real possibility for some people. People can

Walk 10 - 15 blocks to their offices or back home.

Use public transportation, including buses, underground / subways and then walk the rest of the way to their offices or homes.

 

b) Cycling also adds value to the “Let’s walk” proposal for the population 10 to 30 blocks from their office. The Buenos Aires City already has implemented an “ecobici” (eco bikes) program [15]. The ecobici system has bikes parked in several distributed city points, and anyone can use one of these bikes for free using his / her document. You just return the bike in another bike parking place near your destination.

 

REVIEW OF SOME “BIKE TO WORK” PROGRAMS IN CANADA AND USA:

USA Today informs that Bike to Work programs might be rising in US [21].

Through a google search we found the following relevant links:

table-bike-to-work-projects.png


ANSWERS TO SOME OBJECTIONS TO “BIKE TO WORK”

The Huffpost article [16] mentions 9 objections to “Bike to work”, here are our thoughts about them!

main-objections.png

“LET’S WALK BUSINESS MODEL CANVAS”

LET`S WALK PROJECT BUSINESS CANVAS v2.0.png

As we know we have different partners in this event, which play a fundamental role.

On one side, the people enrolled in the event, that will participate registering using a mobile app. They will either walk to their works or commute by bike. In order to be eligible to receive a prize, each participant will allow the app to register information about distances walked / biked, which transportation they usually use to commute in order to calculate GHG emission savings due to the event. Those people that contribute with the biggest GHG emission savings will win prizes as well as  benefits from companies that sponsor the event.

It´s necessary to distinguish, that by incentivizing the movement we are preventing obesity, decrease bad cholesterol and increase good cholesterol, improving the state of mind and incorporating healthy habits, so typically HMOs and the city government will be very much interested, as in Argentina poor people use public hospitals maintained by city taxes.

On the other side, we need to be able to organize this event with which we need the Government's support to convey the event to the society. It will be benefited by an increased use of the public transport by the population, better flow of the traffic (less cars in the streets)  and at the same time it will reaffirm it´s commitment with the environment.

In addition to this, it will be a great opportunity to advertise the use of the eco-bikes(“ecobicis”) [15] and, as a result, the bike lanes.

On the other hand, the information generated by the app (big data)  will be of great utility since it will show which are the population's needs (for example: the best locations for entrances of subways , routes of buses or even which are the areas that are disconnected and need public transport).

It is also important to have the companies that will join on this great event allowing the use of “home office”. In addition, they will collaborate as sponsors and obtain in exchange Big Data to improve its business and find niche.

Sponsorships are one of the best ways of promoting a business and winning popularity between the target and generate good marketing for a brand. It can be specially used to increase credibility, image and prestige of the brand.

All participation is voluntary, and public transportation will still be available to those who want to use it.

LGC Team will ask for suggestions and advice from NGOs(such as WWF, who implemented "The earth hour"). In order to have more tools to guide the team on how to develop the project. 
 
Once LCG Team recieves feedback from NGOs.They will present the formal proposal to the Institute of Technology of Buenos Aires (ITBA) and to the MIT Colab
 
The team is looking forward to receive feedback from CLIMATE COLAB in order to improve the project. ITBA will give their consent and support to it.
 
We received the recognition and prize from MIT Colab thanks to the backing through votes from ITBA.
 
LGC Team will contact several organizations to help with fundraising and to add value to the final event (GOOGLE, MASS MEDIA, SPONSORS, etc). Nevertheless to make it happen we need firstly the GOVERNMENT authorization.
 
The government will notificate the companies of the proposal and ask for their cooperation by allowing the employees to work from home. They will also have to arrange with the transportation about the modification of their routes.
 
Distance walked and GHG savings: To have an estimation of the GHGs saved is necessary an app (develop by GOOGLE if possible) that will measure them.The application may use a similar alogorithm that "calculate your ecological footprint" uses, that is available at the Government web page.But it is important to keep record of the route like Google Maps does. 
 
Once the proposal is accepted the next step is to settle a date and communicate it to the society, a key actor in this process is MASS MEDIA. 
 
The day of the event SPONSORS will play an important role by motivating the population to walk more. This could be achived by offering refreshments and prizes. 
 
Finally,all the information is collected by GOOGLE. Including steps per person and the equivalence with the reduction of GHGs obteined.
 
The LGC Team will analyze the results and make papers to be published and to communicate it to the community, the government and the MIT. 
 
As the results will be positive this idea can be implemented in other countries, all over the world. By this the proposal will reach a greater impact and a change in people's habits worldwide.


Who will take these actions?
LGC Team at ITBA: designs proposal and presents it to Climate Colab.
 
Argentine Government sponsors "LET'S WALK day"
 
Media covers the events publishing metrics on energy saved, emissions saved and money saved.
 
5 % local city population participates in the "LET'S WALK day"
 
NGOs sponsor event.
 
Transportation works as usual.

Other countries can implement the proposal to have a higher impact
Where will these actions be taken?
City level: The first objective is to do it in Buenos Aires City as is the most populated region, the most contaminating one and the principal concentration of companies.

Country level: Later by the positive results our goal is to carry out the experience at country level, in cities like Cordoba, Rosario, Mendoza and San Luis.

Worldwide: Finally, other countries can use the proposal to make their own impacts since everything counts.

 

In addition, specify the country or countries where these actions will be taken.
Argentina

Country 2
Brazil

Country 3
Chile

Country 4
United States

Country 5
No country selected

Impact/Benefits
What impact will these actions have on greenhouse gas emissions and/or adapting to climate change?
1) City of Buenos Aires

a) Short term CO2e emissions saved in Buenos Aires city:

We postulate that 5 % of the population will participate in the event.
Public transportation use: 80% of population [1]

 
Emissions saved by cars commuting from Buenos Aires Province:

10410352 cars/month [2] /30 days/month x 15 km/trip  x 0.1 kg CO2eq / km [3] x 0.05 ( 5 % population participates  in event) = 26 tCO2e (metric tons) / day.
 

Emission saved by cars inside the city:

2.89 million people x 5% people participation x 20 % people use cars x 7 km average trip to work x 2 trips /day x 0.1 kg CO2eq / km [3] = 40.46 tCO2e / day
 

Total short term CO2e savings = (26 + 40.46) = 66.46 tCO2e /day

b) Long term CHG savings (5 years) in Buenos Aires city:

We forecast that recycling could increase by 10 %. Today only 23% of Buenos Aires city recycle [4]. And recycling saves from 0.34 to 10 Tonnes of CO2e from each ton of recycled material [5]. Considering an average 1 Tonne of CO2e saved per Tonne of recycled material, current Buenos Aires savings from recycling would be 976 Tonnes of domiciliary residues [6] x 23% people recycle x 0.1 (10 % recycling increase ) x 1 tCO2e/1 t recycled material = 22 Tonnes of CO2e / year saved.
 
Energy savings recommendations [7] use increment by 10 %. The average electricity consumption per electrified household in Argentina is 3600 kWh [11], so we estimate that the savings will be of 360 kWh/home per year * 963 thousand homes * 0.05 (5 % of participation) = 17334 kWh per year. This could save up to 17334 kWh x 0.0005925 tCO2e/kWh = 10.2 tCO2e/year
Distributed solar photo voltaic electricity law is approved and covers 1 % of city demand.
2.89 million people x 4100 kWh/person [10] x 0.01 (1% of demand) x 0.0005925 tCO2e/kWh = 70205 tCO2e/year
 
It incentivates the use of public transport in this way, so the Government must make it more efficient.For example, the use of electric buses is implemented in 2 % of city.[8] [9] Each bus will save 175 metrics tones of pollutant emissions and 22.700 liters of fuel per unit and year. We estimate that there are 10200 buses in Buenos Aires,so 2% is 204, the total reduction will be 204*175 mtones= 35700 metric tones of pollutant / year saved.[10]
 
Total emission savings = (22 + 10.2 + 70205 + 35700) = 105937 tCO2e/year

2) Extrapolation to world:

a) Short term:

66.46 tCO2e/(2.89million people) x (1087 million people) = 24997 tCO2e

b) Long term:

105.937 tCO2e/year /(2.89million people) x (1087 million people) = 39 million tCO2e / year.

The transportation sector accounts from 19 % to 25 % of total GHGs emissions, according to [12] and [13]

So the total GHG due to the transportation sector may be calculated as

46.000 million tCO2e x 22% = 10.120 million tCO2e

So the proposal savings would be 39 / 10120 = 0.38 % of the transportation sector emissions.

What are other key benefits?
Generate awareness on using more public transport
Government will have to improve the efficiency of public transport
Change the habits of the society towards a sustainable and ecological life
Smog and sound pollution will be reduced
People´s stress levels will be reduced
Increase the use of bicycles
Change habits and weaken sedentary lifestyle
Cut costs of transport and so combustibles
By implemeting home-office besides helping this cause, the employeers will have a cut down of their costs
Decrease GHGs emissions by reducing the use of cars and public transport
Costs/Challenges
What are the proposal’s projected costs?
1) Cost of security forces involved in organizing the event.

2) Cost of health support personnel, ambulances, paramedics,

3) Cost of traditional media ads

4) Cost of blog, SN ads and  social media managers / influencers

5) Cost of winner prizes

6) Cost of collaboration agreement with smartphone apps that calculate distances walked by people participating in the event

7) Cost of webpage that summarizes energy savings, money saved, GHGs emission saved.

Timeline
The day of the event, there will be an immediate impact by the reduction of the GHGs emissions.
In the short-term we are looking forward to make people realize that their can make a huge change on a daily basis.For example, they can avoid the use of cars in short distances.
Over more, we can affirm that one of important change will be the use bikes. Nowadays, Buenos Aires city provides free bikes and bicicles in the downtown. Our idea is to massify these kind of habits and became a green country free of sedentarism.
What's more, we want to Incentive companies to stablish a home-office day. This will allow people who live far from their jobs to colaborate to this cause.
Medium-term: These "Homeoffice days" could at first be once a year, but once people catch the habit, it could be done monthly.
Long-term: Eventually people could do these "Homeoffice days" weekly, so that people enjoy life a little more and savings of energy, money and GHGs emissions are multiplied.

feasibility.rationale: The proposal relies on a 'perfect storm' of unverified assumptions: 5% of the city's population voluntarily walking/biking to work on a single day, simultaneous government authorization for route modifications, and corporate adoption of home-office policies without economic incentives. The technical feasibility of the tracking app is low; the proposal suggests using an existing 'ecological footprint' algorithm (designed for static annual estimates) to calculate real-time, route-specific GHG reductions, which is a category error in data modeling. Furthermore, the logistical requirement for 'security forces' and 'ambulances' for a walking event in a dense city suggests a misunderstanding of the risk profile, inflating operational complexity.

novelty.rationale: The core concept—a 'Walk to Work' or 'Bike to Work' day—is a well-established global practice (e.g., Earth Day, Bike to Work Day) with no novel mechanism for behavior change. The proposal attempts to differentiate itself via a smartphone app and 'Big Data' analytics, but the proposed data utility (identifying disconnected areas) is a standard feature of existing transit planning tools (e.g., Google Maps, Citymapper) and does not require a new, event-specific application. The 'business model' of selling data to sponsors is generic and lacks a unique value proposition.

impact.rationale: The quantitative impact claims are scientifically unsound and likely grossly inflated. The short-term calculation (66.46 tCO2e/day) assumes a 5% participation rate of the entire population (including non-workers) shifting modes, which is empirically unlikely for a one-off event. The long-term projections are logically flawed: the proposal attributes massive systemic changes (e.g., 5% city demand covered by solar, 2% electric bus fleet) to a single awareness day. Causal mechanisms linking a one-day event to a 10% increase in recycling or the approval of specific legislation are non-existent; these are policy outcomes driven by years of advocacy, not a single event. The extrapolation to '39 million tCO2e/year' globally is a mathematical error that ignores regional heterogeneity in transport infrastructure and behavior.

presentation.rationale: The proposal suffers from significant structural and technical deficiencies. It contains numerous typos ('algoritm', 'kilometrers', 'obteined'), inconsistent units, and broken references to images (e.g., 'Main value generation loops v1.0.png') that are not provided. The logical flow is disjointed, jumping from event logistics to speculative long-term policy outcomes without establishing the causal chain. The reliance on 'Google' to develop a custom app and the assumption that the government will 'modify routes' based on a single day's data demonstrates a lack of understanding of institutional and technical constraints.

A1.perspective_summary: From a systems and empirical evidence perspective, this proposal is a classic case of 'solutionism' where a complex systemic problem (climate change) is addressed with a simplistic, one-off behavioral intervention that lacks a robust causal mechanism for long-term change. The quantitative modeling is fundamentally flawed, conflating short-term event participation with long-term structural shifts in energy and transport infrastructure. While the intent to raise awareness is valid, the proposal fails to demonstrate scientific plausibility regarding its projected impacts or operational feasibility.
key_concerns: Causal Fallacy: The proposal assumes a one-day event can causally drive multi-year policy changes (solar law approval, electric bus implementation) and behavioral shifts (10% recycling increase) without intermediate mechanisms.

Data Modeling Error: Using a static 'ecological footprint' algorithm for dynamic, route-specific real-time tracking is technically invalid; the proposal lacks a defined methodology for accurate GHG calculation per trip.
Participation Overestimation: The 5% population participation rate for a voluntary walking/biking day in a major metropolis is empirically unsupported and likely orders of magnitude too high.

Systemic Interdependencies: The proposal ignores the friction of institutional change (government route modifications, corporate home-office mandates) required to make the event viable, treating them as guaranteed outcomes.
Extrapolation Flaw: Global impact calculations ignore regional differences in transport modes and infrastructure, rendering the '39 million tCO2e' figure scientifically meaningless.

feasibility.rationale: The proposal relies on a 'magic wand' governance assumption: that the government will unilaterally mandate home office, re-route public transport, and authorize a massive data collection event without a legislative or administrative framework. The claim that the government will 'arrange with transportation about the modification of their routes' for a single day is operationally unrealistic for a major transit system. Furthermore, the reliance on a Google-developed app and the assumption of voluntary corporate compliance for home office days ignores the significant legal and labor constraints in Argentina. The governance capacity to coordinate this level of cross-sectoral alignment (health, transport, tech, media, labor) for a one-off event is not demonstrated.

novelty.rationale: The core concept—a 'Walk to Work' or 'Bike to Work' day—is a well-established global practice (e.g., Earth Hour, Bike to Work Day in the US/Canada, which the proposal itself cites). The novelty is limited to the specific context of Buenos Aires and the proposed use of a smartphone app for tracking. However, the proposal does not offer a new governance mechanism or a novel policy instrument; it is a standard awareness campaign wrapped in a 'big data' promise that lacks technical and regulatory grounding.

impact.rationale: The quantitative impact claims are severely flawed and likely inflated due to a lack of regulatory realism. The proposal extrapolates a one-day event's short-term savings into long-term structural changes (e.g., a 10% increase in recycling, 5% solar coverage, 2% electric bus implementation) without explaining the policy levers or budgetary mechanisms required to achieve these. The calculation that a single day of walking will lead to the approval of a 'Distributed solar photovoltaic electricity law' is a non-sequitur. The governance gap between a 'walk day' and systemic infrastructure change is ignored, rendering the long-term impact projections speculative and ungrounded in policy reality.

presentation.rationale: The proposal is structured with clear sections and attempts to use data, but the presentation of the data is misleading. It presents complex, multi-year policy outcomes (solar law approval, electric bus rollout) as direct, calculable consequences of a single awareness event. The inclusion of 'Business Model Canvas' and 'Objections' sections shows an attempt at rigor, but the underlying logic fails to address the political and institutional constraints necessary to make the proposal viable. The tone is optimistic but lacks the critical analysis of implementation barriers expected in a governance-focused proposal.

A2.perspective_summary: From a governance and regulatory perspective, this proposal is a classic case of 'solutionism' where a behavioral nudge is expected to trigger complex systemic policy changes without the necessary institutional scaffolding. While the intent to raise awareness is valid, the proposal fails to account for the political economy of transport, labor laws regarding home office, and the administrative burden of coordinating a city-wide event. The reliance on voluntary corporate action and government 'support' without a clear mandate or funding mechanism makes the execution highly improbable.
key_concerns: The proposal assumes the government can and will re-route public transport and mandate home office days without a legal framework or budgetary allocation.

The long-term impact projections (solar law, electric bus rollout) are presented as direct outcomes of a one-day event, ignoring the years of legislative and capital investment required for such changes.

Data privacy and sovereignty concerns are unaddressed: relying on a Google app to track citizen movement and health data for a government-sanctioned event raises significant regulatory red flags in Argentina and globally.

The 'voluntary' nature of the event conflicts with the proposal's expectation of mass participation (5% of the city) and the resulting traffic/transportation impacts, which would require active enforcement or incentives not detailed.
The proposal conflates 'awareness' with 'policy implementation,' assuming that public pressure from a single day will automatically result in the approval of complex laws (e.g., solar PV law).

feasibility.rationale: The operational feasibility is critically low due to reliance on unverified government mandates (forcing companies to allow home office, modifying bus routes) and a 'big tech' dependency (Google building a custom app) that is not grounded in reality. The proposal assumes a one-day event can trigger complex systemic changes (electric bus rollout, solar law approval) without a phased implementation plan or budget for the necessary security and health infrastructure.

novelty.rationale: The concept of 'Walk to Work' or 'Bike to Work' days is a well-established global practice (e.g., Earth Hour, Bike to Work Day). The proposal adds a smartphone tracking layer, but the core mechanism is a standard awareness campaign. The novelty is further diminished by the lack of a unique value proposition beyond existing municipal programs like 'Ecobici'.

impact.rationale: The quantitative impact claims are mathematically unsound and logically flawed. The proposal extrapolates a single day's event into long-term structural changes (e.g., 2% electric bus implementation, 10% recycling increase) without a causal mechanism. The calculation of 39 million tCO2e/year for the world is a gross over-extrapolation of a one-day local event, ignoring behavioral decay and the difference between a 'pilot' and a 'policy shift'.

presentation.rationale: The presentation is fragmented and relies heavily on placeholder text (e.g., 'main-objections.png', 'table-bike-to-work-projects.png') rather than substantive analysis. The business model canvas and impact calculations are presented as assertions rather than evidence-based projections. The tone is overly optimistic and lacks the rigor required for a scalable implementation plan.

A3.perspective_summary: From an implementation standpoint, this proposal is a classic 'awareness event' masquerading as a systemic solution. It fails to address the hard constraints of operational logistics, funding, and behavioral economics. The assumption that a single day of walking will force government policy changes or corporate home-office mandates is operationally naive. The impact metrics are inflated to the point of being misleading, and the reliance on external entities (Google, Government) to execute core components without a clear contract or budget makes the project non-viable as described.
key_concerns: The proposal assumes the Argentine Government will mandate companies to allow home office and modify public transport routes based on a single event; this is a massive political and operational barrier with no mitigation strategy.
The impact calculations conflate a one-day behavioral nudge with long-term infrastructure investment (e.g., electric buses, solar laws), creating a false causal link that will not survive scrutiny.
There is no budget or resource plan for the 'security forces,' 'health support,' and 'prizes' listed in the costs section; the proposal assumes these will be provided for free by sponsors.

The reliance on Google to develop a custom tracking app is a critical single point of failure; no major tech company builds custom apps for one-off NGO events without a commercial or strategic partnership.
The scalability argument (extrapolating Buenos Aires data to the entire world) ignores the vast differences in urban density, infrastructure, and culture between cities.

feasibility.rationale: The proposal relies on a 'magic bullet' assumption that the government will mandate companies to allow home office and that transportation routes will be modified for a single-day event. In reality, logistics for a city-wide shift in commuting patterns require months of coordination, not just a 'notification.' Furthermore, the reliance on a custom Google app to track specific GHG savings for a one-off event is technically and financially unrealistic for a volunteer team; existing apps (Strava, Google Maps) already do this, and the proposal's plan to build a new one creates a high barrier to entry and data fragmentation.

novelty.rationale: The concept of 'Walk to Work' or 'Bike to Work' days is a well-established global practice (referenced in the proposal itself via US/Canada examples). The proposal adds little new methodology, merely repackaging existing awareness campaigns with a specific focus on Buenos Aires. The 'novelty' of using an app to track steps is non-existent in the current market, and the proposal does not offer a unique mechanism for engagement beyond standard gamification.

impact.rationale: The impact calculations are dangerously optimistic and logically flawed. The proposal claims a 5% population participation rate for a voluntary one-day event, which is statistically improbable without massive infrastructure support. More critically, the long-term impact model assumes a single day of walking will trigger a 10% increase in recycling and a 2% adoption of electric buses over five years; this causal link is tenuous and ignores the complex policy and economic drivers required for such systemic changes. The 'Big Data' value is overstated as the data collected (steps walked) is already available through existing municipal and private datasets.

presentation.rationale: The proposal is structured with clear sections and attempts to use data, but the tone is overly idealistic and disconnected from the gritty reality of urban mobility. It reads like a theoretical exercise rather than a grounded plan. The inclusion of broken image links (e.g., 'main-objections.png') and the reliance on 'Google' to build a custom app without a technical roadmap undermines the professional credibility of the presentation.

A4.perspective_summary: From the ground, this proposal feels like a 'feel-good' event that ignores the structural barriers preventing people from walking or biking. It assumes that if we just ask people to walk, they can, ignoring safety, distance, heat, and the reality of low-income workers who cannot afford to skip a day of work or travel long distances on foot. The proposal treats the city as a blank slate rather than a complex, often hostile environment for pedestrians.
key_concerns: Accessibility Exclusion: The proposal assumes everyone can walk 10-15 blocks or bike 30 blocks. This ignores people with disabilities, the elderly, parents with strollers, and those with physically demanding jobs who cannot simply 'skip the bus' without risking their livelihood or health.

Safety and Infrastructure: Buenos Aires has significant gaps in safe pedestrian and cycling infrastructure. Encouraging mass walking/biking without addressing traffic violence or lack of sidewalks could lead to accidents, not awareness.

The 'Home Office' Fallacy: The proposal assumes companies will allow home office for a 'climate day.' For many service, manufacturing, and care workers, this is impossible. This creates a class divide where only white-collar workers can participate, alienating the very populations most affected by pollution.

Data Privacy and Trust: The proposal asks citizens to track their movements via a new app for 'Big Data' to be sold to sponsors. In a post-pandemic world, communities are wary of surveillance; this could be perceived as extractive rather than empowering.

Unintended Consequences: If 5% of the population stops using public transport for a day, it might actually disrupt the system's efficiency or create a false sense of security about emissions, distracting from the need for permanent, structural transit improvements.

feasibility.rationale: The proposal relies on a 'magic bullet' assumption that government and corporate actors will unilaterally restructure work schedules (mandatory home office) and transport routes based on a single awareness day. The text admits the government must 'arrange with the transportation about the modification of their routes,' a logistical impossibility for a one-day event without massive, pre-existing infrastructure changes. Furthermore, the reliance on a Google-developed app and the assumption that 5% of the population will voluntarily walk 10-15km in a dense city without significant safety or time incentives renders the operational model highly fragile.

novelty.rationale: The core concept—'Bike/Walk to Work' days—is a well-established global practice (explicitly referenced in the text regarding US/Canada programs). The proposal adds little new normative or structural innovation, merely repackaging existing awareness campaigns with a 'Big Data' tracking layer that does not fundamentally alter the behavioral mechanism or address the root causes of car dependency.

impact.rationale: The proposal hides a severe distributional injustice under the guise of 'altruism.' By demanding people 'skip the bus' and 'turn to their own bodies,' it implicitly excludes low-income workers who rely on public transit for long commutes, the elderly, and those with disabilities. The claimed long-term impacts (e.g., 10% recycling increase, 5% solar adoption) are causally unconnected to a single walking event and represent a 'halo effect' fallacy. The calculation of 39 million tCO2e saved globally is mathematically unsound, extrapolating a one-day local event to a global annual figure without accounting for the rebound effect or the fact that 95% of the population will continue their usual high-carbon routines the next day.

presentation.rationale: The proposal is structured with standard sections (Business Canvas, Impact calculations), but the narrative is riddled with internal contradictions (e.g., 'skip the bus' vs. 'incentivize public transport') and vague, unverified assumptions. The tone is overly optimistic and lacks critical engagement with the structural barriers to walking in a major metropolis like Buenos Aires, presenting a 'feel-good' narrative that obscures the lack of a concrete implementation strategy.

A5.perspective_summary: This proposal prioritizes symbolic visibility over structural justice, masking a 'one-size-fits-all' approach that risks excluding the most vulnerable populations who cannot simply 'walk' to work. It conflates a one-day awareness stunt with systemic behavioral change, ignoring the economic and physical realities of the working class. The value conflict is stark: the proposal demands personal sacrifice from individuals while expecting systemic transformation from governments without a credible mechanism to enforce or fund it.
key_concerns: Exclusion Risk: The mandate to 'skip the bus' and walk long distances disproportionately penalizes low-income workers, the disabled, and those with caregiving responsibilities, effectively making climate action a privilege of the able-bodied and geographically fortunate.

False Causality: The proposal assumes a single day of walking will trigger complex, long-term policy shifts (solar laws, electric bus fleets) without providing a mechanism for how this causal link is established.

Data Privacy vs. Public Good: The proposal relies on a Google app to track citizen movement and sell 'Big Data' to sponsors, raising significant ethical concerns about surveillance capitalism disguised as environmentalism.

Rebound Effect: There is no plan to address why people drive in the first place (lack of safe infrastructure, time poverty), meaning the 'saved' emissions are likely temporary and will rebound immediately after the event.

Normative Trade-off: The proposal frames 'walking' as the only moral choice, stigmatizing necessary public transit use and ignoring that in many contexts, public transit is the most sustainable option compared to walking long distances in unsafe conditions.

feasibility.rationale: The proposal relies on a fragile chain of dependencies: government authorization, corporate cooperation for home-office, and a custom app development by a major tech giant (Google) that is not guaranteed. The assumption that a single-day event will trigger complex systemic changes (e.g., 2% of bus lines converting to electric, new solar laws) within 5 years is operationally unrealistic without a dedicated, multi-year policy framework, not just an awareness day.

novelty.rationale: The core concept—a 'Walk to Work' or 'Bike to Work' day—is a well-established global practice (referenced in the proposal itself regarding US/Canada). The addition of a tracking app and the specific context of Buenos Aires does not constitute a novel systemic intervention, but rather a replication of existing awareness campaigns with a data-tracking layer.

impact.rationale: The proposal conflates a one-day behavioral shift with long-term structural transformation. The claimed long-term impacts (e.g., 10% increase in recycling, 2% electric bus fleet conversion) are causally disconnected from a single day of walking; these require legislative and infrastructural overhauls that an awareness event cannot force. The direct GHG savings (66 tCO2e/day) are negligible on a global scale, and the extrapolation to '39 million tCO2e/year' assumes a permanent habit change that the proposal offers no mechanism to sustain beyond the event.

presentation.rationale: The proposal is structured with clear sections and attempts quantitative analysis. However, the logic is flawed: it presents speculative policy outcomes (solar laws, electric bus adoption) as direct 'long-term impacts' of the event itself. The reliance on image placeholders (e.g., 'main-objections.png') and the assumption that Google will build a custom app for a student-led initiative undermines the professional credibility of the presentation.

A6.perspective_summary: From a long-term resilience and systems perspective, this proposal is a classic 'awareness trap.' It mistakes a temporary reduction in emissions for a structural solution. The proposal fails to address the systemic barriers (urban design, infrastructure, economic incentives) that prevent walking or cycling, relying instead on a 'nudge' that is unlikely to alter deep-seated mobility patterns. The claimed long-term societal shifts (recycling rates, energy laws) are presented as inevitable consequences of the event, which is a dangerous oversimplification of how systemic change occurs.
key_concerns: Causal Fallacy: The proposal assumes that a one-day awareness event will directly cause complex legislative and infrastructural changes (e.g., electric bus adoption, solar laws) within 5 years without a detailed policy roadmap.

Resilience vs. Shift: The event may simply shift travel times or modes temporarily without building the permanent infrastructure (safe bike lanes, walkable neighborhoods) required for long-term resilience.

Data Dependency: The entire impact measurement relies on a custom app developed by a third party (Google) that has no stated commitment, creating a single point of failure for the project's core value proposition.

Exaggerated Extrapolation: The calculation of global impact (39 million tCO2e) assumes that a one-day event in Buenos Aires will result in a permanent, global behavioral shift, ignoring the high rate of habit reversion after such events.

feasibility.rationale: The proposal rests on a fragile causal chain: a single-day behavioral nudge (walking) is assumed to trigger complex, multi-year systemic shifts (solar law approval, electric bus rollout) without intermediate policy mechanisms. As peers A2 and A3 correctly identified, the assumption that the government will unilaterally re-route transit or mandate home-office days for a one-off event lacks administrative plausibility. The technical feasibility of a custom Google app for a single event is also low; relying on a major tech giant for a non-commercial, one-day data collection tool is a critical single point of failure. The 'magic wand' governance assumption significantly lowers the probability of execution.

novelty.rationale: The core intervention—'Walk/Bike to Work' days—is a mature, globally replicated practice (e.g., Earth Hour, Bike to Work Day). The proposal's attempt to differentiate via 'big data' tracking does not constitute a novel mechanism; it merely digitizes a standard awareness campaign. The reliance on existing 'Ecobici' infrastructure further reduces novelty. There is no new scientific principle or unique system intervention proposed, only a re-packaging of established behavioral nudges.

impact.rationale: The quantitative impact projections are scientifically unsound due to a fundamental category error: conflating a one-day event with long-term structural policy outcomes. The calculation that a single day of walking will lead to a 5% solar coverage or 2% electric bus implementation is a non-sequitur lacking causal evidence. The extrapolation of local, short-term data to global, long-term metrics (39 million tCO2e/year) ignores behavioral decay, infrastructure lag times, and the difference between correlation and causation. The short-term savings (66 tCO2e) are negligible on a city scale, and the long-term claims are speculative fiction rather than evidence-based forecasting.

presentation.rationale: The proposal is structurally organized with clear sections (Business Model Canvas, Impact/Benefits), which aids readability. However, the presentation is undermined by the inclusion of placeholder images ('main-objections.png') and the presentation of highly speculative long-term policy outcomes as direct, calculable consequences of the event. While the format is professional, the content lacks the rigorous evidence anchoring required for a scientific evaluation, presenting optimistic assumptions as facts.

A1.perspective_summary: From a systems and empirical evidence perspective, this proposal fails to establish a plausible causal mechanism between a one-day awareness event and the complex, long-term systemic changes it predicts. The reliance on unverified government mandates and the conflation of short-term behavioral data with long-term infrastructure policy represent significant scientific and logical gaps. While the intent to promote active transport is valid, the proposal's impact claims are not grounded in the reality of policy implementation timelines or behavioral economics.
key_concerns: Causal Fallacy: The proposal assumes a direct causal link between a one-day walking event and the approval of complex legislation (solar law) and infrastructure rollout (electric buses), ignoring the years of legislative and capital investment required.

Governance Gap: There is no evidence or mechanism provided for how the government would unilaterally mandate home-office days or re-route public transit for a single event, rendering the operational plan unrealistic.

Data Validity: The global impact extrapolation (39 million tCO2e/year) is mathematically derived from a single-day local event without accounting for behavioral decay, scaling friction, or the difference between a pilot and a systemic shift.
Technical Dependency: The reliance on a custom app developed by Google for a one-off event is a critical vulnerability with no demonstrated path to execution.

Measurement Error: The proposal conflates 'awareness' with 'emission reduction,' assuming that the act of tracking steps automatically translates to the structural changes required for the projected long-term savings.

feasibility.rationale: The proposal is institutionally non-viable as written. It relies on a 'perfect storm' of unverified assumptions: the Argentine government mandating corporate home-office policies and modifying public transport routes based on a single day's data, and Google developing a custom app without a commercial contract. As a governance expert, I must flag that the proposal confuses 'voluntary awareness' with 'regulatory authority.' The request for government authorization to 'modify routes' and 'notify companies' to change work policies represents a fundamental misunderstanding of public administration and labor law. The logistical burden of security and health support for a walking event in a dense metropolis further inflates the operational risk to an unmanageable level without a dedicated budget.

novelty.rationale: The core intervention—a 'Walk/Bike to Work' day—is a globally saturated practice with no novel governance mechanism. The proposal attempts to differentiate via a 'Big Data' app, but the data utility (identifying disconnected areas) is already a standard function of existing transit planning tools (e.g., Google Maps, Citymapper). The 'business model' of selling data to sponsors is generic. While the specific context of Buenos Aires is noted, the mechanism itself offers no new institutional leverage or policy innovation compared to existing 'Ecobici' programs or global 'Earth Hour' campaigns.

impact.rationale: The impact projections are scientifically and logically unsound, rendering the proposal's value proposition illusory. The causal chain is broken: a one-day awareness event cannot causally drive multi-year policy outcomes like the approval of a 'Distributed Solar Photovoltaic Electricity Law' or a 2% shift to electric buses. The quantitative modeling is flawed, extrapolating a single day's participation (5% of the population) into long-term structural changes and global emissions reductions (39 million tCO2e/year) without accounting for behavioral decay or regional heterogeneity. The proposal conflates short-term behavioral nudges with systemic infrastructure transformation, a critical error in impact assessment.

presentation.rationale: The presentation fails to meet the standards of a professional policy proposal. It contains numerous technical errors ('algoritm', 'kilometrers'), broken image references, and disjointed logical flow. The reliance on placeholder images and the lack of a coherent budget or risk mitigation strategy undermine its credibility. The tone is overly optimistic, presenting speculative outcomes as guaranteed results, which is inappropriate for a document seeking institutional support and funding.

A2.perspective_summary: From a governance and regulatory feasibility lens, this proposal is a classic case of 'solutionism' that ignores the hard constraints of institutional capacity and political reality. It attempts to solve complex systemic problems (climate change, urban mobility) with a simplistic, one-off event that lacks the regulatory teeth, budget, or causal mechanisms to achieve its stated long-term goals. The reliance on unverified government mandates and tech giant goodwill makes it operationally impossible to execute as described.
key_concerns: Regulatory Overreach: The proposal assumes the government can and will mandate corporate home-office policies and modify public transport routes based on a single event, which is politically and legally unrealistic.
Causal Fallacy: There is no logical mechanism linking a one-day walking event to the approval of complex legislation (solar law) or the deployment of electric bus fleets.

Data Integrity: The proposal lacks a defined methodology for accurate GHG calculation, relying on a static 'ecological footprint' algorithm for dynamic, real-time tracking, which is technically invalid.
Resource Vacuum: There is no budget or resource plan for the listed costs (security, health support, prizes), assuming these will be provided for free by sponsors or the government.
Scalability Error: The global impact extrapolation ignores the vast differences in urban infrastructure and culture, rendering the '39 million tCO2e' figure meaningless.

feasibility.rationale: I maintain a low feasibility score (2) despite peer alignment, as the operational barriers are insurmountable without a fundamental pivot. The proposal demands a 'perfect storm' of coordination: government route modifications, corporate home-office mandates, and a custom Google-developed app for a single-day event. As an implementation expert, I see the 'security forces and ambulances' requirement for a walking event as a critical misallocation of resources that signals a lack of risk assessment. The reliance on a static 'ecological footprint' algorithm for real-time, route-specific tracking is a technical failure that invalidates the core data value proposition. The cost of coordinating these disparate stakeholders (transport, health, tech, media) for a one-off event vastly outweighs the likely return on investment.

novelty.rationale: The proposal offers no novel mechanism. 'Walk to Work' and 'Bike to Work' days are ubiquitous global practices (e.g., Earth Day, Bike to Work Day). The proposed 'Big Data' angle is a red herring; existing transit planning tools (Google Maps, Citymapper) already possess superior route and usage data. The idea of selling this data to sponsors is a generic business model with no unique value proposition. The proposal attempts to dress a standard awareness campaign in 'innovation' clothing without adding any new operational or technological capability.

impact.rationale: The impact projections are scientifically unsound and operationally disconnected from reality. The short-term calculation assumes a 5% population participation rate for a voluntary event, which is empirically unrealistic for a major metropolis. More critically, the long-term impact claims (e.g., a one-day event causing a 10% recycling increase, the approval of a solar law, or a 2% electric bus fleet implementation) represent a fundamental causal fallacy. These are structural policy outcomes requiring years of legislative and capital investment, not the result of a single awareness day. The extrapolation to '39 million tCO2e' globally ignores regional heterogeneity in infrastructure, rendering the figure meaningless for strategic planning.

presentation.rationale: The presentation fails to meet professional standards for an implementation proposal. It is riddled with typos ('algoritm', 'kilometrers'), broken image references, and inconsistent units. The logical flow is disjointed, jumping from event logistics to speculative policy outcomes without establishing a causal chain. The reliance on 'Google' to build a custom app and the assumption that the government will modify routes based on a single day's data demonstrates a profound lack of understanding of institutional constraints and technical feasibility. The 'Business Model Canvas' included is superficial and does not address the actual cost drivers or risk mitigation strategies.

A3.perspective_summary: From an operational feasibility and risk management perspective, this proposal is a high-risk, low-reward initiative. It conflates a one-day behavioral nudge with complex systemic policy changes, ignoring the massive institutional friction required to execute the plan. The technical approach to data collection is flawed, the cost structure is undefined and likely inflated by unnecessary security measures, and the impact projections are mathematically and causally invalid. While the intent to raise awareness is positive, the proposal lacks the rigorous planning required for real-world deployment.
key_concerns: Operational Overreach: The requirement for security forces and ambulances for a walking event indicates a severe misjudgment of risk and resource allocation.
Technical Debt: The proposed app relies on a static algorithm for dynamic tracking, which is a category error that will result in inaccurate data and wasted development costs.
Causal Fallacy: The proposal assumes a single event can trigger multi-year legislative and infrastructure changes (solar law, electric buses) without intermediate policy mechanisms.
Stakeholder Friction: The plan assumes voluntary compliance from corporations and government route changes without a clear incentive structure or legal framework.
Data Integrity: The 'Big Data' value proposition is weak as it duplicates existing capabilities of major transit apps without offering superior granularity or utility.

feasibility.rationale: I stand by the low feasibility score. The proposal's reliance on a 'Google-developed app' and the assumption that the government will 'arrange with transportation' for a one-day event is operationally naive. From the ground, asking a single mother in Villa 31 to walk 15km to work because 'cars should be left at home' ignores the reality of her commute, safety risks, and lack of time. The logistical friction of coordinating home-office mandates across thousands of SMEs without a binding policy framework makes this unworkable as described.

novelty.rationale: My score remains 2. As my peers noted, 'Bike to Work' and 'Walk to Work' days are global staples. The proposal adds a 'Big Data' layer, but from a community perspective, this feels like surveillance rather than innovation. It does not solve the core problem of why people *can't* walk (unsafe streets, long distances, heat) but merely asks them to try anyway for a day. It repackages existing awareness campaigns without addressing the structural barriers that make walking inaccessible for the working poor.

impact.rationale: I am lowering my impact score from a tentative 2 to a 1 based on the peers' rigorous critique of the 'halo effect' and distributional injustice. The proposal explicitly asks people to 'skip the bus' and 'turn to their own bodies,' which is a direct attack on the mobility of low-income residents, the elderly, and the disabled. The claimed long-term impacts (solar laws, electric buses) are causally unconnected to a single day of walking; this is a dangerous fantasy that distracts from real policy needs. The '39 million tCO2e' global extrapolation is mathematically absurd and ignores the rebound effect. The proposal risks stigmatizing necessary public transit use while offering no safety net for those who cannot walk.

presentation.rationale: The presentation is structurally complete but substantively hollow. It uses standard business canvas sections to mask a lack of critical engagement with the lived reality of Buenos Aires. The internal contradiction of 'skipping the bus' while claiming to 'incentivize public transport' is a major red flag. The reliance on image placeholders and the assumption that Google will build a custom app for a student project undermines credibility. It presents a 'feel-good' narrative that obscures the lack of a concrete, inclusive implementation strategy.

A4.perspective_summary: This proposal is a classic example of 'climate privilege' disguised as altruism. It demands physical sacrifice from the most vulnerable populations (who cannot walk long distances or work from home) while expecting systemic miracles from governments and corporations without a credible mechanism. The 'skip the bus' directive is particularly harmful, as it frames public transit as an enemy rather than a necessary, low-carbon solution for the majority. The proposal fails to account for the safety, time, and economic realities of the working class, making it not just ineffective, but potentially exclusionary and harmful.
key_concerns: Exclusionary Mandate: The instruction to 'skip the bus' and walk long distances effectively bans low-income workers, the disabled, and caregivers from participating, turning climate action into a privilege of the able-bodied and wealthy.

False Causality: The proposal assumes a one-day event will trigger complex, long-term legislative changes (solar laws, electric bus fleets) without any policy roadmap, creating a dangerous disconnect between the event and the claimed outcomes.

Surveillance Capitalism: The reliance on a Google app to track citizen movement and sell 'Big Data' to sponsors raises severe ethical concerns about privacy and the commodification of community data under the guise of environmentalism.

Rebound Effect & Safety: There is no plan to address the root causes of car dependency (unsafe infrastructure, time poverty), meaning the 'saved' emissions are temporary. Furthermore, encouraging walking in unsafe areas without infrastructure improvements puts participants at risk.

Stigmatization of Public Transit: By framing 'skipping the bus' as a moral imperative, the proposal undermines the very public transport systems that are the most sustainable option for the majority of the city's population.

feasibility.rationale: The proposal's feasibility is critically undermined by its reliance on a 'magic bullet' assumption: that a single-day event can trigger complex, multi-year policy shifts (e.g., electric bus adoption, solar laws) and that major tech giants (Google) will voluntarily build custom infrastructure for a student-led initiative. As peers A4 and A6 correctly noted, the logistical chain—government mandates, corporate home-office approvals, and app development—is too fragile. From a justice perspective, the feasibility is further compromised because the plan assumes a level of agency and flexibility (e.g., skipping work, walking long distances) that is structurally unavailable to low-income and essential workers, making the 'voluntary' nature of the event a barrier to actual participation for the most vulnerable.

novelty.rationale: The proposal offers no novel mechanism for justice or inclusion. It repackages the well-established 'Walk/Bike to Work' model, which has historically struggled to address systemic inequities. The addition of a tracking app does not constitute a new value proposition; rather, it risks commodifying citizen movement data without a clear ethical framework for who owns or benefits from that data. The proposal fails to innovate on *how* to engage excluded populations, instead relying on a 'one-size-fits-all' approach that ignores the diverse realities of urban mobility.

impact.rationale: The impact assessment is deeply flawed due to a 'causal fallacy' that conflates a one-day behavioral nudge with long-term structural transformation. The proposal claims that a single day of walking will lead to a 10% increase in recycling and a 2% shift to electric buses over five years; this ignores the complex political and economic drivers required for such changes. More critically, the distributional consequences are negative: the 'benefits' (health, awareness, prizes) accrue primarily to those with the privilege to walk or bike (white-collar workers), while the 'costs' (disruption of public transit, safety risks, data extraction) are borne by the broader public and marginalized groups who cannot participate. The projected GHG savings are negligible and distract from the need for systemic policy reform.

presentation.rationale: The presentation is structurally clear but normatively naive. It uses quantitative data to create an illusion of rigor while hiding the ethical and practical gaps in the logic. The reliance on broken image links and the assumption of Google's involvement without a formal agreement undermines credibility. Crucially, the presentation fails to address the 'who benefits, who bears the cost' question, presenting a sanitized version of urban mobility that excludes the realities of disability, poverty, and safety.

A5.perspective_summary: This proposal is a classic example of 'awareness washing' that masks deep structural inequities. It assumes that individual behavioral change is sufficient to solve climate change, ignoring the systemic barriers that prevent the most vulnerable from participating. The proposal's reliance on a single-day event to trigger long-term policy shifts is not just optimistic; it is a distraction from the hard work of building inclusive, resilient infrastructure. The 'Big Data' component raises serious concerns about data extraction and privacy, particularly for marginalized communities.
key_concerns: Exclusionary Design: The proposal assumes all citizens have the physical ability, time, and economic flexibility to walk or bike, effectively excluding people with disabilities, the elderly, parents with young children, and low-income workers who cannot afford to skip work or travel long distances.

Data Colonialism: The plan to collect movement data via a custom app for 'Big Data' to be sold to sponsors risks exploiting citizen participation without ensuring data sovereignty or equitable benefit sharing.

Causal Fallacy: The proposal falsely attributes long-term systemic changes (e.g., electric bus adoption, solar laws) to a single-day awareness event, ignoring the complex policy and economic drivers required for such transformations.

Safety and Infrastructure: Encouraging mass walking/biking in a city with inadequate pedestrian and cycling infrastructure (as noted in Buenos Aires) could increase the risk of accidents and violence, particularly for vulnerable populations.

False Equivalence: The proposal treats 'walking' and 'biking' as equivalent options for all, ignoring the reality that for many, public transit is the only viable option, and skipping it for a day could disrupt essential services or livelihoods.

feasibility.rationale: The proposal's feasibility is critically undermined by its reliance on a 'one-day miracle' to trigger systemic change. As peers A4 and A5 correctly identified, the assumption that a single event can force government route modifications or corporate home-office mandates is logistically naive. From a long-term resilience perspective, a system dependent on voluntary, one-off behavioral shifts without structural infrastructure (safe sidewalks, protected bike lanes) is fragile. The plan to build a custom app when existing data streams exist further reduces feasibility, creating a high-risk, low-reward implementation path.

novelty.rationale: The proposal repackages established 'Walk/Bike to Work' campaigns without introducing new mechanisms for systemic resilience. The peers are correct that the 'Big Data' layer adds no novel value; it merely digitizes a standard awareness stunt. True novelty in sustainability requires shifting from individual behavior modification to structural adaptation. This proposal remains stuck in the 'individual responsibility' paradigm, failing to offer a new model for how cities can structurally integrate low-carbon mobility.

impact.rationale: The impact assessment is fundamentally flawed and potentially counter-productive to long-term goals. The peers' critique of the 'halo effect' is decisive: assuming a single day of walking will causally drive a 10% increase in recycling or the approval of solar laws is a logical fallacy that ignores the complex, multi-decadal nature of policy change. Furthermore, the proposal's instruction to 'skip the bus' actively undermines the most effective low-carbon transport mode (public transit) in favor of walking, which is often less efficient for longer distances and excludes vulnerable populations. This creates a 'rebound effect' where short-term symbolic gains mask long-term systemic stagnation. The extrapolation to 39 million tons of CO2e is mathematically unsound and dangerously misleading.

presentation.rationale: While the structure mimics a professional proposal, the content reveals a disconnect from the reality of urban systems. The inclusion of broken image links and the reliance on 'Google' to solve technical problems without a roadmap suggests a lack of rigorous planning. More importantly, the presentation fails to address the second-order effects highlighted by peers: the exclusion of low-income workers and the safety risks of mass walking in an unprepared city. A presentation that ignores these systemic vulnerabilities cannot be considered effective for a long-term impact expert.

A6.perspective_summary: This proposal represents a classic 'awareness trap': it prioritizes a visible, one-day event over the invisible, long-term work of building resilient infrastructure. From a systems lens, it fails because it treats symptoms (individual commuting choices) without addressing the root causes (lack of safe infrastructure, car-centric urban planning). The peers' identification of the 'skip the bus' fallacy is crucial; it reveals a proposal that could actively harm public transit viability while offering negligible long-term climate benefits. The causal links between a walking day and major policy shifts (solar laws, electric buses) are non-existent, rendering the long-term impact claims speculative at best and misleading at worst.
key_concerns: Systemic Fragility: The proposal relies on voluntary, temporary behavior change rather than building permanent, resilient infrastructure (e.g., protected bike lanes, safe sidewalks).
Counter-Productive Messaging: The directive to 'skip the bus' undermines the most efficient mode of mass transit, potentially reducing overall system efficiency and excluding those who cannot walk.

False Causality: The assumption that a single event will trigger complex, long-term policy changes (recycling rates, solar adoption) ignores the decades-long timelines required for such systemic shifts.

Exclusion and Inequity: The proposal implicitly excludes low-income workers, the elderly, and people with disabilities, creating a 'climate privilege' divide that weakens social cohesion and long-term resilience.

Data Misuse: The plan to collect and sell 'Big Data' on citizen movement raises ethical concerns about surveillance and extractive capitalism, which could erode public trust in future sustainability initiatives.

feasibility.rationale: The cluster unanimously identifies the proposal as institutionally and operationally non-viable. The plan relies on a 'perfect storm' of unverified assumptions: the Argentine government mandating corporate home-office policies and re-routing public transit based on a single day's data, and Google developing a custom app without a commercial contract. The requirement for security forces and ambulances for a voluntary walking event signals a critical misjudgment of risk and resource allocation. The causal chain linking a one-day event to complex legislative outcomes (solar law approval) is logically broken, rendering the execution probability near zero.

novelty.rationale: The core intervention—a 'Walk/Bike to Work' day—is a globally saturated, mature practice with no novel governance mechanism. The proposed 'Big Data' differentiation is a red herring; existing transit planning tools (Google Maps, Citymapper) already possess superior route and usage data. The business model of selling data to sponsors is generic. While the specific context of Buenos Aires is noted, the mechanism offers no new institutional leverage or policy innovation compared to existing 'Ecobici' programs or global 'Earth Hour' campaigns.

impact.rationale: The impact projections are scientifically unsound and logically invalid. The proposal commits a fundamental category error by conflating a one-day behavioral nudge with long-term structural policy outcomes. The claim that a single day of walking will trigger the approval of a 'Distributed Solar Photovoltaic Electricity Law' or a 2% shift to electric buses is a non-sequitur lacking any causal evidence. The extrapolation of local, short-term data to global, long-term metrics (39 million tCO2e/year) ignores behavioral decay, infrastructure lag times, and regional heterogeneity, rendering the figures meaningless for strategic planning.

It is riddled with technical errors (e.g., 'algoritm', 'kilometrers'), broken image references, and disjointed logical flow. The document presents highly speculative long-term policy outcomes as direct, calculable consequences of the event, undermining its credibility. S1.synthesis: The cluster (A1, A2, A3) reached a high degree of consensus, with no substantive disagreements requiring resolution. All three agents identified the same critical failures: the 'causal fallacy' linking a one-day event to multi-year legislative changes, the 'governance gap' regarding government mandates, and the 'technical dependency' on a custom Google app. A1 and A3 initially scored Feasibility at 2, while A2 scored it 1; however, upon reviewing the 'institutional non-viability' and the 'regulatory overreach' arguments, the cluster agreed that the operational barriers are insurmountable without a fundamental pivot, justifying the lowest score of 1. Similarly, while A1 gave Presentation a 3, A2 and A3 gave it 2; the cluster resolved this by prioritizing the presence of broken images, typos, and the presentation of speculation as fact, lowering the score to 2. The synthesis reflects a unified view that the proposal is a case of 'solutionism' that ignores the hard constraints of institutional capacity and political reality.
S1.uncertainty: There are no remaining disagreements among the cluster members regarding the fundamental flaws of the proposal. The only residual uncertainty lies in the hypothetical scenario where the proposal is radically re-scoped: if the long-term policy claims were removed and the event treated strictly as a short-term awareness campaign with a realistic budget and no reliance on government mandates, the feasibility and impact scores could theoretically improve. However, as currently written, the judgment is robust and definitive.

feasibility.rationale: The proposal is operationally unworkable due to its reliance on unrealistic dependencies: a custom app built by Google for a student project, government-mandated route changes for a single day, and voluntary home-office adoption across thousands of SMEs. The logistical friction of coordinating these elements without a binding policy framework renders the plan naive and likely to fail in execution.

novelty.rationale: The concept repackages existing 'Walk/Bike to Work' campaigns without introducing new mechanisms for systemic change. The addition of a tracking app does not constitute innovation but rather adds a layer of surveillance to a standard awareness stunt. It fails to address the structural barriers (safety, infrastructure, distance) that prevent walking, offering no novel solution to the core problem of urban mobility inequity.

impact.rationale: The impact assessment is fundamentally flawed and potentially counter-productive. The proposal relies on a 'causal fallacy,' assuming a one-day event will trigger complex, long-term legislative changes (solar laws, electric bus fleets) without a policy roadmap. Furthermore, the directive to 'skip the bus' actively undermines the most efficient low-carbon transport mode, stigmatizing public transit and excluding vulnerable populations. The claimed GHG savings are mathematically unsound and ignore the rebound effect.

presentation.rationale: While the proposal uses a standard business canvas structure, the content is substantively hollow and normatively naive. It relies on broken image links and unverified assumptions (e.g., Google's involvement) to mask a lack of critical engagement with the lived reality of the target population. The presentation fails to address the 'who benefits, who bears the cost' dynamic, presenting a sanitized view that obscures significant ethical and practical gaps.

S2.synthesis: The cluster (A4, A5, A6) reached a strong consensus that the proposal is critically flawed, though the intensity of the critique varied slightly. The primary disagreement was not on the final scores but on the *nature* of the failure: A4 focused on the 'climate privilege' and exclusionary nature of the 'skip the bus' mandate; A5 emphasized the 'causal fallacy' and 'data colonialism' risks; A6 highlighted the 'systemic fragility' and lack of resilience. These perspectives were integrated by recognizing that the proposal fails simultaneously on ethical, logical, and operational grounds. The 'skip the bus' directive was identified as the most damaging element, as it contradicts the goal of sustainable transport by discouraging the most efficient mode (public transit) and excluding those who cannot walk. The 'Big Data' component was universally rejected as a non-innovative, ethically dubious add-on. The final scores reflect a synthesis where the proposal is deemed not just ineffective, but potentially harmful to the very goals it claims to support. The 'average' score of 1.75 reflects the cluster's view that the proposal is fundamentally broken across all dimensions, with Impact receiving the lowest score due to the dangerous disconnect between the event and the claimed long-term outcomes.
S2.uncertainty: While the cluster agrees on the low scores, there remains a minor divergence on the *degree* of harm. A4 and A6 lean towards a score of 1 for Impact, viewing the proposal as actively harmful due to its exclusionary nature and stigmatization of public transit. A5 assigned a 2, acknowledging the harm but perhaps leaving a tiny margin for the 'awareness' aspect, however flawed. The cluster has resolved this by settling on 1, as the 'skip the bus' directive and the false causality of the long-term claims outweigh any potential symbolic value. Another unresolved question is whether the 'Big Data' component could be salvaged if decoupled from the 'skip the bus' mandate, but given the current proposal's structure, this remains a theoretical possibility rather than a practical path forward.

feasibility.rationale: The proposal is institutionally and operationally non-viable. It relies on a 'perfect storm' of unverified assumptions: the Argentine government mandating corporate home-office policies and re-routing public transit based on a single day's data, and Google developing a custom app without a commercial contract. The requirement for security forces and ambulances for a voluntary walking event signals a critical misjudgment of risk and resource allocation. The causal chain linking a one-day event to complex legislative outcomes is logically broken, rendering the execution probability near zero.

novelty.rationale: The core intervention—a 'Walk/Bike to Work' day—is a globally saturated, mature practice with no novel governance mechanism. The proposed 'Big Data' differentiation is a red herring; existing transit planning tools already possess superior route and usage data. The business model of selling data to sponsors is generic. While the specific context of Buenos Aires is noted, the mechanism offers no new institutional leverage or policy innovation compared to existing 'Ecobici' programs or global 'Earth Hour' campaigns.

impact.rationale: The impact projections are scientifically unsound and logically invalid. The proposal commits a fundamental category error by conflating a one-day behavioral nudge with long-term structural policy outcomes. The claim that a single day of walking will trigger the approval of a 'Distributed Solar Photovoltaic Electricity Law' or a 2% shift to electric buses is a non-sequitur lacking any causal evidence. Furthermore, the directive to 'skip the bus' actively undermines the most efficient low-carbon transport mode, potentially causing net negative impact by stigmatizing public transit.

presentation.rationale: The presentation fails to meet professional standards for a policy proposal. It is riddled with technical errors (e.g., 'algoritm', 'kilometrers'), broken image references, and disjointed logical flow. The document presents highly speculative long-term policy outcomes as direct, calculable consequences of the event, undermining its credibility. The reliance on placeholder images and the lack of a coherent budget or risk mitigation strategy further weaken the proposal's professional standing.

final.synthesis: The synthesis of S1 (A1-A3) and S2 (A4-A6) reveals a unified consensus on the proposal's fundamental failure, though the clusters approached the critique from slightly different angles. S1 focused heavily on the 'causal fallacy' and 'institutional non-viability,' while S2 emphasized the 'ethical hazards' of the 'skip the bus' directive and the 'data colonialism' risks. The primary disagreement to resolve was whether the 'awareness' value could salvage the Impact score. S2's A5 initially considered a score of 2 for Impact, acknowledging potential symbolic value, whereas A4 and A6 argued for a 1 due to the active harm of discouraging public transit. I resolved this by siding with the lower score (1) because the proposal's core logic actively contradicts established climate science (public transit is more efficient than walking for longer distances) and the long-term claims are not just optimistic but logically impossible. The 'Big Data' component was universally rejected as non-innovative. The Feasibility score was settled at 1 (the floor) because the reliance on government mandates and a custom Google app for a student project represents a total lack of operational reality. The Presentation score was lowered to 2 to reflect the unprofessional nature of broken links and typos, overriding any initial leniency from S1. The final judgment is that the proposal is a case of 'solutionism' that ignores hard constraints and promotes counter-productive behaviors.
final.uncertainty: The only remaining uncertainty lies in the hypothetical scenario where the proposal is radically re-scoped: if the long-term policy claims were removed, the 'skip the bus' directive was replaced with 'walk for short trips, take transit for long,' and the event was treated strictly as a short-term awareness campaign with a realistic budget and no reliance on government mandates, the feasibility and impact scores could theoretically improve. However, as currently written, the judgment is robust. A minor unresolved question is whether the 'Big Data' component could be salvaged if decoupled from the flawed event structure, but given the current proposal's structure, this remains a theoretical possibility rather than a practical path forward.
